perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate. In the event of breach by RECIPIENT, HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, to the extent remedies are permitted by the governing law.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'to the extent remedies are permitted by the governing law' this introduces ambiguity and undermines the explicit agreement to equitable remedies like injunctions. This weakens the enforceability of HALO's rights, contradicting the intent of specifying remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 outlines situations where an injunction may be granted. By including the clause 'to the extent remedies are permitted by the governing law,' the modified text introduces uncertainty about the availability of injunctive relief, even in situations where § 3422 would permit it, undermining the original intent for readily available injunctions.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the laws most favorable to the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the laws most favorable to the recipient creates an imbalance and potential contradiction of California law, which should govern the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 deals with unconscionable contracts or clauses. A clause stating that the contract will be interpreted under the laws most favorable to one party can be deemed unconscionable, especially if it leads to unfair or one-sided outcomes, which is a potential violation of this code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO. The determination on returning the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be determined at the discretion of the RECIPIENT.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the ownership of physical forms/copies of confidential information to being returned or destroyed at the Recipient's discretion contradicts the protection of Halo's IP.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By giving the recipient discretion over the return or destruction of confidential information copies, the modification increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure, which violates the spirit and intention of protecting trade secrets as outlined in the Act. The original text was intended to protect against that. This modified text enables the recipient to not comply and the change has diminished the owner's control over their secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and RECIPIENT will use reasonable care to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase '(but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own,' the level of care required is reduced and is open to misinterpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1644 discusses the interpretation of contracts, implying that the language should be interpreted reasonably, and such that performance is completed in good faith. Removing the specification that the RECIPIENT will take at least reasonable care may imply that the RECIPIENT can take less than reasonable care, undermining the reasonable interpretation standards and good faith requirements in contract performance. The original text specified that their care should be equal or better than the care they take for their OWN information, which is a strong statement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.original_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any license or right to RECIPIENT by HALO under any patent of the United States or a foreign country or otherwise (except for the evaluation purposes indicated above).
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting any license or right to RECIPIENT by HALO under any patent of the United States or a foreign country or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By removing '(except for the evaluation purposes indicated above),' we enable the Recipient to infer they have rights for other than evaluation purposes.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 271 defines patent infringement. By removing the explicit allowance for evaluation purposes, the modified text creates potential ambiguity. While the agreement still states no license is granted, the omission of the evaluation exception could arguably expose the recipient to infringement claims if they use the information for purposes beyond simple evaluation, but would have otherwise been allowed. It creates an opening for a legal challenge based on implied rights, which wasn't present in the original text.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 271 - Infringement of patent | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 271 - Infringement of patent U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Except as otherwise provided in this title, whoever without authority makes, uses, offers to sell, or sells any patented invention, within the United States or imports into the United States any patented invention during the term of the patent therefor, infringes the patent. (b) Whoever actively induces infringement of a patent shall be liable as an infringer. (c) Whoever offers to sell or sells within the United States or imports into the United States a component of a patented machine, manufacture, combination or composition, or a material or apparatus for use in practicing a patented process, constituting a material part of the invention, knowing the same to be especially made or especially adapted for use in an infringement of such patent, and not a staple article or commodity of commerce suitable for substantial noninfringing use, shall be liable as a contributory infringer. (d) No patent owner otherwise entitled to relief for infringement or contributory infringement of a patent shall be denied relief or deemed guilty of misuse or illegal extension of the patent right by reason of his having done one or more of the following: (1) derived revenue from acts which if performed by another without his consent would constitute contributory infringement of the patent; (2) licensed or authorized another to perform acts which if performed without his consent would constitute contributory infringement of the patent; (3) sought to enforce his patent rights against infringement or contributory infringement; (4) refused to license or use any rights to the patent; or (5) conditioned the license of any rights to the patent or the sale of the patented product on the acquisition of a license to rights in another patent or purchase
